stem: Solve \( x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) = 0 \) by factoring. What is the smaller of its two real solutions?
solution: Factoring gives \( (x-([[r1]]))(x-([[r2]])) = 0 \), so the two solutions are \( [[r1]] \) and \( [[r2]] \); the smaller one is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the LARGER of the two solutions, not the smaller one.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign, and is also the larger root.
This multiplies the two roots (their product) instead of reporting either root.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Which of the following is a value of \( x \) that satisfies \( [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) = 0 \)?
solution: Factoring out the leading coefficient, \( [[a]](x-([[r1]]))(x-([[r2]])) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \), so the equation's solutions are \( x=[[r1]] \) and \( x=[[r2]] \); one value that satisfies it is \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by one from one of the two actual solutions.
This is off by one from the other actual solution.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is the PRODUCT of the two solutions, not a solution itself.
This is off by three from the correct value.
This is off by three from the correct value.

stem: A quadratic equation has two real roots whose sum is [[S]] and whose product is [[P]]. Writing the equation as \( x^{2} - ([[S]])x + ([[P]]) = 0 \) and applying the quadratic formula, what is the smaller of the two roots?
solution: Substituting the sum and product into \( x^{2}-(\text{sum})x+(\text{product})=0 \) gives \( x^{2} - ([[S]])x + ([[P]]) = 0 \), which factors as \( (x-([[r1]]))(x-([[r2]])) = 0 \); the smaller root is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the LARGER of the two roots, not the smaller one.
This equals the larger root re-derived clumsily from the sum, not the smaller root.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is an arbitrary combination of the given sum and product, not a root.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Consider \( [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) = 0 \). Using the quadratic formula, what is the smaller of its two real solutions? (The answer need not be rational; give the exact value.)
solution: By the quadratic formula, \( x = \dfrac{-([[b]]) \pm \sqrt{([[b]])^{2}-4([[a]])([[c]])}}{2([[a]])} \). Since \( [[a]] > 0 \), the smaller root uses the minus sign: \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the PLUS sign, giving the larger root instead of the smaller one.
This has the wrong sign under the radical (adds instead of subtracts \( 4ac \)).
This forgets the factor of 2 in the denominator.
This drops the negative sign on \( b \) in the numerator.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The equation \( [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) = 0 \) has two real roots. What is the sum of its roots?
solution: By Vieta's formulas, for \( [[a]]x^{2}+([[b]])x+([[c]])=0 \) the sum of the roots is \( -\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to negate the ratio \( b/a \).
This gives the PRODUCT of the roots, \( c/a \), not their sum.
This gives the negative of the product of the roots, not their sum.
This multiplies the coefficients \( a \) and \( b \), which has no meaning here.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

stem: The equation \( [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) = 0 \) has two real roots. What is the product of its roots?
solution: By Vieta's formulas, for \( [[a]]x^{2}+([[b]])x+([[c]])=0 \) the product of the roots is \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This negates the ratio \( c/a \), which is the correct product but with the wrong sign.
This gives the negative of the SUM of the roots, not their product.
This gives the sum of the roots itself, not their product.
This multiplies the coefficients \( a \) and \( c \), which has no meaning here.
This is off by one from the correct product.
This is off by one from the correct product.

stem: Solve \( x^{3} + ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) = 0 \) by factoring. What is the smallest of its three real solutions?
solution: Factoring gives \( (x-([[r1]]))(x-([[r2]]))(x-([[r3]])) = 0 \), with solutions \( [[r1]], [[r2]], [[r3]] \); the smallest is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the middle solution, not the smallest one.
This is the largest solution, not the smallest one.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A rectangle's length is [[d]] units more than its width. The rectangle's area is [[A]] square units. If \( w \) denotes the width, this gives the equation \( w^{2} + ([[d]])w - ([[A]]) = 0 \). Which of the following is a value of \( w \) that satisfies this equation?
solution: The equation factors as \( (w-([[w0]]))(w+([[w0]])+([[d]])) = 0 \), so \( w = [[w0]] \) or \( w = -([[w0]])-([[d]]) \); either value satisfies the equation, e.g. \( w = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by one from the width solution.
This is off by one from the width solution.
This is just the given difference between length and width, not a solution to the equation.
This is just the given area, not a solution to the equation.
This is off by one from the other (negative) solution.
This is off by two from the correct value.
